title: Production Chain Organization in the Digital Age: Information Technology Use and Vertical Integration in U.S. Manufacturing
abstract: Advances in information technology (IT) may affect the organizational design of production. Exploiting the rapid diffusion of the internet in the United States, we assess the sensitivity of production chain organization to this innovation in IT access and use. Extending theories of the firm that recognize the importance of downstream transfers (selling as opposed to sourcing) and plural governance in organizational design, we predict IT-driven shifts in downstream vertical integration. In a detailed panel of Census Bureau data for over 5,600 manufacturing plants, we observe the extent of a production unit’s downstream transactions within the firm alongside concurrent sales to external customers—a mix we refer to asplural selling. Our main finding is that the use of the internet for external coordination precipitated a significant decline in downstream vertical integration across the manufacturing sector. Instrumental variables estimation points to a causal relationship but also heterogeneous treatment effects. Key drivers of plural organization, such as complementarities and constraints across differently governed transactions, help explain such heterogeneity, as does concurrent use of internal production management IT. Our study is the first study to leverage a plural governance framework and large-scale microdata to understand how U.S. production chain organization shifted in response to this rapid and far-reaching technological change.

author.Chris Forman: Charles H. Dyson School of Applied Economics and Management, Cornell SC Johnson College of Business, Cornell University, Ithaca, New York 14853
author.Kristina McElheran: University of Toronto Scarborough Department of Management, University of Toronto, Scarborough, Ontario M1C 1A4, Canada; and Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto, Toronto, Ontario M5S 3E6, Canada

article.1. Introduction: Understanding the economic implications of new technologies requires consideration not only of improvements to existing processes, but also of how they may be redesigned. Steam power and electricity transformed production in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, but only after widespread economic reorganization (Rosenberg and Trajtenberg 2004,Jovanovic and Rousseau 2005). Digital technologies arguably sparked a similar upheaval in this century (David 1990,Brynjolfsson and McAfee 2014), yet many organizational implications remain poorly understood. We explore how the rise of the internet—a central catalyst of today’s digital transformation—affected the vertical organization of production in U.S. manufacturing. Long-standing research in management and economics contends that by lowering communication and coordination costs, information technology (IT) may alter the organizational design of firms (Malone et al. 1987,Bresnahan et al. 2002,Brynjolfsson and Hitt 2003,Bloom et al. 2014). Particular attention has been paid to whether IT use might lead firms to become less vertically integrated.1More-recent work finds a mixed relationship between IT and vertical organization depending on a firm’s external context (Ray et al. 2009), asset characteristics (Ray et al. 2013), or the nature of the IT (Dewan and Ren 2011,Gong et al. 2016). We explore IT-related changes in the organization of vertical transactions from a novel theoretical perspective. This perspective, combined with largely untapped establishment-level data on transaction flows across the U.S. manufacturing sector and detailed measures of IT use, yields new insights into how digital coordination may substitute for ownership-based control in modern production. We find that internet-led shocks to external value chain coordination resulted in a substantial reduction in downstream vertical integration. Heterogeneity across plants (even within the same firm) as well as across distinct uses of IT sheds new light on how firms adopt, combine, and adapt to novel technologies. Our study complements prior research in a few key ways. To begin, we ground our study in plural governance theory, which seeks to explain firms’ simultaneous use of “distinct control mechanisms for the same function” (Bradach and Eccles 1989, p. 97). In other words, this framework does not neatly characterize firms as integrated, not-integrated, or even “hybrid” (Williamson 1991,Ménard 2012) across similar activities, and it is consistent with the diversity of governance structures observed in practice (Sørensen et al. 2022). This departs from the widely used transaction cost economics (TCE) framework (Coase 1937,Williamson 1985), which examines organizational design through the lens of a “make-versus-buy” decision at the transaction level. TCE pushes predictions toward “pure” firm types that rely on one governance mode or the other (Parmigiani 2007,Puranam et al. 2013). Plural governance theory, in contrast, admits much greater diversity and accommodates the vital insight that organizational design will be shaped not only by transaction-level features but also by cross-transaction interdependencies and past design choices (Argyres and Liebeskind 2002,Leiblein and Miller 2003,Nickerson and Silverman 2003,Jacobides and Hitt 2005,Argyres and Zenger 2012,Puranam et al. 2013). Grounding our study in plural governance leverages these advances in the theory of the firm and opens up new avenues of inquiry. One such avenue is attention to the production-unit level of analysis. Key drivers of plural forms, such as information and incentive complementarities across governance modes (Dutta et al. 1995,Heide 2003), operate at the level of a “system” or “portfolio” of connected activities. Thus, within-firm variation requires attention (Harrigan 1985,Puranam et al. 2013). We exploit establishment-level administrative data to both align with the theory and identify patterns that could be obscured in a firm-level analysis. Large-scale studies of plural organizational forms are uncommon outside of certain settings.2Yet, this design—sometimes referred to as “taper integration” (Harrigan 1985)—is quite prevalent in our research context. U.S. manufacturers predominantly mix vertically integrated downstream transfers with sales to external customers (Atalay et al. 2014), a practice we refer to asplural sellingand take as an apt lens through which to explore shifts in production chain organization. Such shifts are ultimately an empirical question, as the net effect of interdependencies across distinctly governed activities in plural forms is, absent strong assumptions, fundamentally ambiguous (Puranam et al. 2013). Indeed, ambiguity is not new to the IT and vertical design literature; earlier TCE-grounded studies sought to predict the effect of generic IT, which may enhancebothinternal and external coordination, potentially increasingand/ordecreasing vertical integration (Gurbaxani and Whang 1991). This left net organizational design outcomes up to empirical arbitration (Hitt 1999) or required additional information about the external “orientation” of the IT (Dewan and Ren 2011) to resolve. We also disentangle external from internal margins of IT use. This is critical if the internet had a disproportionate impact onexternalcoordination among value chain partners (Afuah 2003). However, even with visibility to the origin of different influences on organizational design, ultimate outcomes are difficult to predict in plural governance contexts. Specifically, the crosstransaction complementarities and constraints giving rise to plural forms generate predictions that are more nuanced than those found in TCE.3Leveraging plural governance theory leads us to develop a number of novel testable propositions that disentangle mechanisms and bring into focus new contingencies to take to the data. For instance, motivated by arguments that plural governance, despite its higher organization and administration costs, may arise from a need for flexibility (e.g.,Chondrakis et al. 2022), we examine whether plants that were plurally governed, ex ante, responded more strongly to the rise of the internet. If plural organization eases reallocation across distinct governance modes, plants organized in this fashion may be better positioned to exploit sudden declines in external coordination costs. In addition, cross-transaction complementarities and constraints central to plural governance theory point to other likely sources of heterogeneity. We thus develop intuitions rooted in this framework that predict variation based on a plant’s pre-internet vertical integration and capacity utilization as well. We further anticipate IT-based heterogeneity, predicting that cross-mode interdependencies will generate stronger external internet-based effects in the presence of internal IT for coordination and management of the production process. However, details of the vintage and nature of the IT systems require care.4Such nuances are usefully captured in our data. Thus, in addition to its theoretical contribution, novel measurements based on detailed U.S. Census Bureau microdata are central to our study. The 1999 Computer Network Use Supplement (CNUS) captured plant-level internet use, separating the sharing of information with external value chain partners from coordination with other company units. It provides data on production-focused IT as well as older IT for external and internal coordination. Rich administrative data further supply key information on plants and their parent firms across the decade that saw the rapid rise of the commercial internet (1992–2002). To assess the effect of internet use on production chain organization, we leverage observed changes over time in the value of output transferred to other plants within the same firm “for further assembly, fabrication, or manufacture.” This measure directly captures output destined for additional transformation in a vertically interdependent production process—an intuitive definition of vertical integration.5Yet, internally governed transfers represent only one side of the plural governance coin; external transfers matter as well. To gauge both magnitude and direction of changes in plural selling across diverse units, we normalize internal transfers by total value shipped by the plant. This “internal transaction ratio” (ITR) captures a plant’s relative reliance on ownership-based control of downstream vertical transactions. We first analyze changes in the ITR in a sample of ∼5,600 plants that consistently pursued plural selling in the pre-period. A one-standard-deviation increase in external coordination over the internet was associated with an ∼8% decline in the ITR on average. This reflects a concomitant 20% decline in the unscaled value of internal transfers (the ITR numerator) and a 4% rise in total value shipped (the ITR denominator) when modeled separately. Instrumental variables, timing falsification, and numerous other tests point to a causal relationship between internet use for external coordination and a significant and widespread decline in vertical integration among U.S. manufacturing plants in the early 2000s. This relationship varied strikingly across units—even within a given firm—based on pre-internet organizational design. First, we find the main effect to be concentrated among units that relied on plural selling, ex ante, consistent with increased flexibility in plural forms despite the higher-cost organizational design. That said, some of the most-constrained plants demonstrated the greatest net adjustments to new IT. Plants characterized by significant vertical linkages to other units within their firms demonstrated a greater IT-relateddecreasein the intensity of vertical integration, shifting a greater share of activity toward market-based transactions. A similar pattern appears with capacity utilization; plants closer to full capacity, pre-internet, allocated relatively more productionoutsidethe firm on net. Such patterns are consistent with strong dependencies across transactions and past unit-level design choices that would be obscured in a standard TCE analysis but that significantly amplified organizational responses to the internet’s introduction of low-cost, flexible coordination throughout the value chain. We find IT-based heterogeneity as well. Concurrent use of IT for monitoring and managing internal production processes accentuates the effects of externally focused use of the internet. This is contrary to common assumptions that external IT and internal IT would act as substitutes in the firm boundary decision (e.g.,Malone et al. 1987,Gurbaxani and Whang 1991). Yet, such complementarity arises only with production management IT. Internet-based information sharing among internal units had no measurable impact on downstream vertical design. Our study contributes to a few streams of work. First, it adds a plural governance frame and evidence of cross-transaction complementarities and constraints to information systems (IS) research on IT and vertical scope. Prior work emphasizes external contingencies (e.g.,Ray et al. 2009), whereas we focus on internal ones rooted in organizational design.Rai et al. (2015)is most related, looking at IT use among plural-sourcing utilities; yet, we find complementarity, rather than substitution, between certain internal and external uses of IT. We also observe patterns in a much broader firm population and shed light on downstream transfers, complementing the predominant focus on sourcing. Finally, evidence that past design choices interact with IT at the unit level to augment organizational responses to IT use is, to our knowledge, new to this literature. It is also relevant to a growing interest in the organizational details and practice of digital transformation (Bharadwaj et al. 2013,Westerman et al. 2014,Vial 2019). More broadly, understanding the organization of economic activity according to the rules of the firm, those of the market, or—as we underscore here—both at once, is vital to management and economics research (Santos and Eisenhardt 2005,Lafontaine and Slade 2007). This literature has also focused predominantly on the discrete “make-versus-buy” decision (e.g.,Nickerson and Silverman 2003,Mayer and Nickerson 2005,Forbes and Lederman 2009) or plural sourcing (e.g.,Jacobides and Hitt 2005,Rothaermel et al. 2006,Parmigiani 2007,Anderson et al. 2019,Chondrakis et al. 2022). Determinants ofdownstreamintegration, a key dimension of vertical structure (Jacobides and Billinger 2006,Kapoor 2013), have been little explored—far less, the impact of IT use. Yet, the potential of digital technologies to shape organizational design is of growing interest (Hubbard 2000;Baker and Hubbard 2003,2004;Bloom et al. 2014;Wu et al. 2019;Kretschmer and Khashabi 2020). Our study yields new insights into the reorganization of U.S. production during a period of important and far-reaching technological change.
article.2. Background and Approach: 2.1. Prior Research on IT and Vertical Integration How IT use impacts the organizational design of firms is a question of long-standing interest. Drawing primarily on TCE (Coase 1937;Williamson 1985,1991), a leading prediction has been that IT investment will lead to less-integrated firms (Malone et al. 1987). This “electronic markets hypothesis” anticipated that IT-driven reductions in external coordination costs would cause firms to increasingly rely on markets, rather than hierarchies, to conduct business.6However, generic IT investment may be used to improve both efficiency and coordination—both within and between firms—yielding ultimately ambiguous predictions (e.g.,Gurbaxani and Whang 1991). Empirical evidence on the topic has likewise been mixed. Industry-level IT capital and firm-level IT capital have been associated with less vertical integration (Brynjolfsson et al. 1994,Hitt 1999) and vice versa (Dewan et al. 1998,Hitt 1999).Ray et al. (2009)find the relationship to depend on a firm’s external market characteristics, whereasRay et al. (2013)find the specificity of a firm’s assets to moderate the relationship. 2.2. Internal vs. External IT Dewan and Ren (2011)address the ambiguity in prior work by developing a contingent view of the relationship between IT and vertical integration depending on the external “orientation” of a firm’s software. This distinction usefully disentangles applications intended to lower the costs of market-based transactions from other IT. Relying on TCE, they argue that externally focused software for customer relationship management or supply chain management tends to complement reduced vertical scope. Such external IT is assumed—both theoretically and by how they construct the measures—to work counter to other enterprise software in its effects on vertical integration. Although we also move beyond generic IT, disentangling external from internal uses, we do not impose substitution between them either conceptually or empirically. We further distinguish the older types of IT examined in their study from the ubiquity, ease of use, and open standards of internet-based IT, which may have distinctive effects on firm boundary decisions (Afuah 2003,Gong et al. 2016). 2.3. Theory-Measurement Gaps Across this body of work, reliance on TCE gives rise to a persistent theory-measurement gap that largely goes unrecognized. TCE takes the transaction as the fundamental unit of analysis, evaluating whether an input would be most efficiently “made” or “bought” in the context of a specific exchange relationship. At the transaction level, this is a discrete choice, with mechanisms that push either toward or away from internal governance. Aggregated across transactions without additional assumptions, TCE predicts “pure” types (Parmigiani 2007), yielding little insight intohow muchshould be made versus bought (Puranam et al. 2013,Anderson et al. 2019). Lacking a way to explain degrees of integration, empirical TCE has often forced a fit between theory and data via arbitrary cutoffs for being “vertically integrated.”7 The empirical IS literature linking IT use to vertical design largely elided this tension, forming predictions based on discrete, transactions-level TCE logic while relying on continuous firm-level measures. Typically, such measures combine verticalscope, defined by breadth of industry participation, withintensityof integration, measured using input-output relationships in the aggregate economy (seehttps://www.bea.gov/). This usefully accounts for interdependencies among steps in the production chain—a key driver of coordination costs in vertical integration (Thompson 1967) and also likely to be impacted by IT use (Dewan and Ren 2011). However, it also introduces a number of challenges. First, it confounds vertical with horizontal scope (Hitt 1999), despite key theoretical and managerial differences (Thompson 1967,Hitt 1999,Dewan and Ren 2011). Second, firms are rarely fully “integrated” according to this metric. Instead, mixing of transaction types dominates in practice, suggesting a need for more nuanced explanations to fit the data. Finally, aggregate input-output relationships are quite stable over time (Atalay et al. 2014). Thus, changes in vertical structure will be observed only in cases of “lumpy” addition or subtraction of entire facilities (Hitt 1999) or shifts across coarsely defined business segments (Villalonga 2004). This may obscure important interdependencies and constraints (Harrigan 1985,Villalonga 2004), calling for higher-fidelity measures as well. 2.4. Production Unit Level of Analysis Our approach narrows the gap between theory and measurement in a few ways. To begin, we model vertical integration in a more fine-grained and flexible manner. In particular, we focus on continuous adjustments in the intensity of vertical integration at the establishment level rather than at the firm level. We note that units within diversified firms often participate in distinct markets with distinct external demand and competition, both of which may influence vertical integration (Ray et al. 2009,Stenbacka and Tombak 2012). Establishments—particularly in manufacturing—also vary in their production functions and productivity (Syverson 2011). This suggests distinct vertical interdependencies and distinct capabilities for participating in distributed value chains (Jacobides and Hitt 2005). Prior work emphasizes system-level or portfolio-level interdependencies that operate at the subfirm level (Rothaermel et al. 2006,Novak and Stern 2009) yet are usefully modeled at the production unit level (Harrigan 1985). Finally, IT use has been found to vary among establishments within firms (McElheran 2014). Thus, a firm-level view could obscure within-firm variation in critical theoretical and empirical relationships. 2.5. Downstream Transactions Another notable feature of our study is that although prior work has focused overwhelmingly onsourcing, both our conceptual framework and empirical measures focus onselling. Many core intuitions readily transfer over, as key information and incentive challenges shaping sourcing decisions similarly affect downstream transfers. For instance, market-based coordination costs, such as searching for appropriate value chain partners, negotiating contract terms, and monitoring contract adherence, arise on both the selling and buying sides of most transactions. In fact, sellers disproportionately bear many classic “marketing” costs, such as communicating product features to potential customers (Coase 1937). “Holdup” risks arising from specialized investments (Williamson 1991), such as customized designs or production equipment, likewise apply as readily to sellers as to buyers. We further justify our downstream view on vertical organization by noting that the costs of coordinating—or failing to—may in fact be greater from the perspective of upstream producers. Long-standing research in operations management recognizes the infamous “bullwhip effect” (Lee et al. 1997,Simchi-Levi et al. 1999), whereby information distortions in supply chains increase with distance from end-user demand. This makes aligning production with demand more difficult the further upstream in the value chain. Thus, manufacturers of intermediate goods for further transformation and producers of final goods for distribution may rely even more heavily on downstream-focused control mechanisms. As in sourcing, ownership provides one lever for exerting downstream control. Forward integration has been studied extensively in retailing (Lafontaine and Shaw 2005,Lafontaine and Slade 2007) and distribution (Dutta et al. 1995). The small-sample manufacturing study ofHarrigan (1985)does not differentiate between backward and forward integration. It does, however, emphasize that firms “need not own a vertically related unit in order to enjoy the advantages vertical control provides” (Harrigan 1985, p. 421). Although we focus on the simple distinction between ownership versus nonownership governance of downstream transactions, many arrangements exist to solve control challenges without co-ownership of facilities (Bradach and Eccles 1989). Some, such as elaborate formal contracts for supply chain coordination, further depend on the capabilities of modern IT (Simchi-Levi et al. 1999,Lee and Whang 2000). 2.6. Beyond TCE: Plural Governance Despite its continued prominence, TCE’s discrete classification of firms into “make,” “buy,” or even “hybrid” (Williamson 1991) has received substantial reformulation over the years. Critics note that firms often makeandbuy the same input (Adelman 1949,Harrigan 1985,Bradach and Eccles 1989). Such plural or “concurrent” sourcing has been broadly documented (Veugelers and Cassiman 1999,Jacobides and Hitt 2005,Parmigiani 2007,Chondrakis et al. 2022), including in studies of partial outsourcing (Parker and Anderson 2002,Stenbacka and Tombak 2012,Anderson et al. 2019).Sørensen et al. (2022)review plural governance research on concurrent sourcing, franchising, and dual distribution. Plural selling, also termed “taper integration” or “vertical permeability,” is noted in a few studies (Harrigan 1985,Jacobides and Billinger 2006,Rothaermel et al. 2006,Luo et al. 2012,Kapoor 2013,Ménard 2013). However, the limited prior work addressing plural selling does so alongside plural sourcing, typically treating them as theoretically equivalent; here, we restrict attention to downstream transactions. We defineplural sellingas a production unit transferring output to downstream units within the firm while simultaneously selling output to external customers. Although much less studied than other plural forms, plural selling is actually quite prominent in U.S. goods-producing sectors. Using large-scale administrative data on downstream shipments,Atalay et al. (2014)find that many producers own links in vertically-related production chains yet transfer only a fraction of output to co-owned facilities. Based on the share of shipments to co-owned locations with significant input-output linkages, only ∼1% of establishments appear fully “vertically integrated” (Atalay et al. 2014, p. 1127). Viewed through a plural governance lens, most can be seen to follow practices consistent with plural selling. We thus take it as an apt theoretical framework for predicting changes in production chain organization during the rise of the internet.
article.3. Conceptual Framework: 3.1. Drivers of Plural Governance Scholars increasingly agree on the stability and benefits of plural forms of organization.8Recapitulating this fast-growing literature here is beyond the scope of our study. Instead, we summarize core theoretical drivers of plural governance, establishing intuitions to take to the data. 3.1.1. Demand for Flexibility. One hypothesized driver of plural forms is a strategic demand for flexibility. For instance, mixing internal and external governance has been associated with the quest to optimize capacity in the face of demand uncertainty or other disruptions (Adelman 1949,Harrigan 1985).Rothaermel et al. (2006)link it to flexibility in product offerings, finding larger product portfolios among computer manufacturers that pursued taper integration in the mid-1990s.Chondrakis et al. (2022)find that plurally governed legal firms were more able to meet new demand for coordination in patent prosecution services. In our manufacturing setting, where plural governance is prevalent but not universal, plants that are plurally governed in pursuit of flexibility may be relatively more responsive to an IT-driven shock. Conversely, those that pursue “pure” governance modes for strategically important reasons—such as full vertical integration to protect valuable intellectual property (Mayer and Nickerson 2005) or to forestall competition (de Fontenay and Gans 2005)—are likely to be relatively insensitive to IT-driven changes in coordination costs. Thus, care is essential at the margins between “pure” and plurally governed types. We build on these insights both in our initial sample construction (seeSection 4) as well as in our exploration of differential responses for plural versus “pure” plant types (Section 6). For the latter, we anticipate a greater response among plants that were engaged in plural selling pre-internet. 3.1.2. Crossmode Complementarities. Numerous studies have detailed the gains from being able to monitor and discipline internal and external activities against each other (e.g.,Bradach and Eccles 1989,Dutta et al. 1995,Stenbacka and Tombak 2012,Ménard 2013,Puranam et al. 2013). Even a small amount of activity in a complementary mode may be sufficient to resolve adverse selection and moral hazard problems (Heide 2003) or to address knowledge challenges, such as learning about input costs or how to integrate discrete components in an overall system (Parker and Anderson 2002,Anderson et al. 2019). Access to distinct sources of innovation and market knowledge are also argued to be important drivers of plural governance (Adelman 1949,Veugelers and Cassiman 1999,Parmigiani 2007,Kapoor 2013). In a detailed case study exploring these themes in practice, the apparel manufacturer “Fashion Inc.” exploited its competences in production and research and development to supply both downstream internal units and external customers, improving monitoring and performance in both modes. Greater vertical “permeability” was also credited with helping it find new downstream market opportunities (Jacobides and Billinger 2006). A central implication is that cross-mode complementarities in plural organizations will generate ambiguous predictions about the relationship between different types of IT use and vertical integration. If improved external IT increases visibility to market demand in ways that also support more productive or more innovative production, it is not clear whether more external sales or more integrated production will prevail. This is not a mere hypothetical. Returning to the “Fashion Inc.” case, the firm relied on ERP software—typically viewed as a tool for internal coordination—to standardize and coordinate orders within the firm. Yet, this also improved how it managed external sales, ultimately reducing its net intensity of vertical integration (Jacobides and Billinger 2006). To the extent that plural governance arises from such cross-mode interdependencies, a given form of enhanced coordination (internal versus external IT) may not be directly responsible for a corresponding increase in that activity (integrated versus external transactions). This clashes with TCE-based intuitions, which impose substitution between internal and external transfers in the firm boundary decision (Parmigiani 2007,Puranam et al. 2013) as well as between internal and external uses of IT (Hitt 1999,Dewan and Ren 2011,Rai et al. 2015). Taking a plural governance view, instead, makes sharp predictions as to average main effects quite tenuous while illuminating new contingencies to explore. In particular, plants with significant vertical linkages within the firm provide an interesting setting in which to explore heterogeneity. Significant internal commitments, pre-internet, could reflect firm-specific investments. According to TCE (e.g.,Williamson 1991), this would tend to constrain a plant’s ability or willingness to transact outside the firm. A plural governance lens, however, suggests that plants having already sunk the costs of “dual” modes (e.g.,Parmigiani 2007) and/or enjoying the insights arising from internal production (e.g.,Anderson et al. 2019) may, in fact, be better positioned to shift their downstream allocations toward external customers. We explore this potential contingency, empirically, inSection 6. 3.1.3. Costs and Constraints. Key organizational costs and constraints, as well, have been argued to drive plural organization. Note that plural selling is more costly than “pure” governance of downstream transfers as it requires the formation and management of distinct governance modes at the same time (Rothaermel et al. 2006,Parmigiani 2007). Net gains from maintaining both modes, therefore, will hinge on an ability to defray those costs, possibly by sharing resources across distinct activities. For a given manufacturing plant, this likely means producing for both internal and external consumption on the same machinery. This can lead to cross-mode competition rather than complementarity, particularly if there are limits to scale in production or administration (e.g.,Coase 1937,Puranam et al. 2013). What does this mean for responses to the internet? It could imply that more-constrained plants will havelesscapacity to reorganize in the face of technological change, dampening the magnitude of a shift toward market-based transactions. However, as with the complementarities described, cross-mode constraints can also generate nonobvious responses to a shift in coordination costs. For example, IT-driven gains in meeting downstream demand could free up capacity to produce more foreitherinternal or external sales—or a mix of both. Given this theoretical ambiguity, we explore heterogeneity based on differences in ex ante production capacity empirically. That said, a larger response among more-constrained plants would be consistent with this difficult-to-observe type of crossmode dependency. 3.2. IT and Coordination Costs in the Internet Age Although organizational design considerations have dominated the development of theoretical mechanisms, IT use has long been argued to reduce coordination and transaction costs both within and between firms (Gurbaxani and Whang 1991). Many applications of IT enhance and/or lower the costs of “making sure that the right things and the right people are in the right place at the right time” (Brynjolfsson et al. 1994, p. 1632). This may be internal, such as improving the flow of information to decision makers or monitoring employees, or external, such as receiving and processing orders from customers. Such intuitions predate the internet. However, the internet’s open, low-cost standards and greater ease of use have been disproportionately linked to improvements in external coordination (Afuah 2003,Gong et al. 2016). Thus, we anticipate the rise of the internet to be most informative about changes in the cost or effectiveness of coordinating with value chain partners. Unlike prior work, we do not need to assume this, as measuring external coordination directly is a key advantage of our data (seeSection 4). 3.2.1. External Coordination. Over the past few decades, IT has transformed the management of vertically linked activities in the value chain. Firms increasingly rely on supply chain and logistics IT to share inventory and order status data directly with vendors and customers, enabling upstream producers to determine production schedules for downstream demand, which reduces both excess inventory and shortages throughout the value chain (Simchi-Levi et al. 1999,Lee and Whang 2000). Before the internet, between-firm coordination using IT relied on proprietary networks, such as EDI. Coordination was only possible between known and approved users, typically already embedded in strategic vertical chain relationships (Bakos and Nault 1997). With the internet, potential (not just existing) partners gained access to product descriptions, prices, and firm attributes as well as visibility into on-hand inventory and real-time logistics outside their firm boundaries. Internet-based IT further improved real-time monitoring and information exchange, helping to find new opportunities for deploying specialized assets and significantly reducing the uncertainty, asymmetric information, and asset specificity underlying many TCE-style “holdup” risks (Afuah 2003). The sudden widespread availability of the internet for external coordination in the mid-1990s, which we labelexternal ITfor simplicity, will have lowered the costs of market-based transactions, all else equal. 3.2.2. Two Kinds of Internal Coordination—and Why They Matter. Yet, despite the internet’s notable ability to facilitate interfirm coordination (Afuah 2003,Brews and Tucci 2004,Gong et al. 2016), its general purpose nature makes it applicable along multiple margins, potentially reducing internal coordination and production costs as well. It is thus important, both conceptually and empirically, to disentangleinterfirmuse fromintrafirmuse of the internet. Because our setting allows us to separate coordination between units of the same firm from other production relationships and activities, we refer to the use of the internet for crossunit coordination within the firm asinternal IT. That said, such symmetry in labeling (“external” versus “internal”) may obscure quite asymmetric magnitudes of change associated with novel internet-based capabilities. Pre-internet, firms typically relied on a range of material requirements planning (MRP) or enterprise resource planning (ERP) software to facilitate internal coordination; such applications were also on the rise across the manufacturing sector in the 1990s and early 2000s (Nguyen and Atrostic 2006,Bai et al. 2018). Use of the internet for internal coordination was also diffusing, although following different patterns than between-firm usage (Forman et al. 2005). Given our interest in shocks to external coordination, we focus first on isolating changes in vertical downstream integration attributable to plant-level adoption ofexternalinternet while partialing out other uses of IT. We can then interact them to explore IT-based complementarities. Along these lines, there is another type of internal IT we must consider. Note that between-unit coordination is conceptually distinct from within-unit coordination. Particularly in the manufacturing context, “shop-floor” applications have been widely available to improve a production unit’s internal production process design, scheduling, and monitoring. Such applications are essential for flexible and/or complex manufacturing as they reduce inventory and downtime from changeovers, inefficient scheduling, or machine failures. Such uses of IT are increasingly important in an age of shortening product life cycles, dynamic e-commerce-driven demand, and globally dispersed supply chains. These applications are often very localized as they require integration with specific production processes—some of which are embodied in shop-floor automation. Thus, they may vary widely among plants, even within a given firm. We label this localized use of ITproduction IT. As mentioned, a central contribution of our study lies in the ability to disentangle these widely ranging uses of IT. This is helpful on two fronts. First, observing what type of IT is deployed and where allows us to pin down the initial locus of organizational change and draw out managerial implications. In other words, it answers open questions about what specific IT capabilities (rather than generic IT investment) might lead to which organizational changes, particularly as organizational adjustment plays out over time. Second, it grants greater precision in linking our measures to common expectations regarding IT use and organizational design—and clarifies where plural governance theory yields novel intuitions. In TCE-based studies, enhanced internal coordination is expected to support greater vertical integration, whereas enhanced external coordination will push toward greater use of hierarchies (e.g.,Malone et al. 1987). Yet, such organizational responses become more nuanced once we employ a plural-selling framework. We return to these nuances in our discussion of heterogeneity. 3.3. Testable Propositions Grounding our analysis within plural governance, therefore, adds insight. Yet, it makes sharp predictions of ultimate outcomes problematic for the reasons described. We make progress conceptually by first noting that net design outcomes arise from a process of organizational adjustment that begins with an initial shift—one that remains intuitive in light of prior work. Drawing on the IT productivity literature (e.g.,Brynjolfsson and Hitt 2003), we anticipate that all else equal—particularly, barring any demand or production capacity constraints—total output should increase with the adoption ofexternal IT. This is a statement about output, yet it has testable implications with respect to organizational design depending on the maintained assumptions. Consider that if the level of internal activity were to be held fixed, increasing activity (sales) on the external margin would cause the relative intensity of vertically integrated activity to decrease on net. This could be a reasonable expectation in certain cases: for instance, if only a small amount of internal activity is needed to apply competitive pressure to external value chain partners (Dutta et al. 1995) or sustain systems integration knowledge (Anderson et al. 2019). Under such assumptions,external ITuse would feed entirely into increased sales, reducing the relative intensity of vertical integration as captured by the ITR. External IT use will lead to a reduction in ITR without meaningful changes in the level of internally governed transfers. The conditions under which this “denominator-only” pattern will hold are relatively restrictive, however, and they conflict with how complementarities between internal and external activities are commonly discussed in plural governance theory. A strict interpretation of complementarity predicts increasing returns to one activity in the increasing level of the other (Brynjolfsson and Milgrom 2013). Thus, in the presence of such cross-mode complementarities,external ITuse should lead to increases inbothexternal and internal transfers. This could potentially leave the ITR—and hence, the observed intensity of downstream vertical integration—relatively unaffected. In this case, cross-mode complementarities will have a “countervailing” effect. External IT use will lead to increases in both external and internal transfers while leaving the observed ITR relatively unaffected. Little (or even no) effect represents a lower bound on the range of absolute-value changes in the ITR that could result from shifts in both the numerator and the denominator of the ITR. We address this by investigating changes in internal transfers and external sales separately as well. Finally, as discussed, constraints across governance modes or time may also play an important role in plural governance. Indeed, they could promote an even greater observed shift in the vertical organization of production by forcing a reallocation of activity that had previously been internal to the firm out to the market. An extreme case of this in “pure” modes would be shifting from fully vertically integrated to fully outsourced, requiring considerable divestment or acquisition of facilities, with commensurate frictions (e.g.,Monteverde and Teece 1982). A more incremental version is possible in plural selling, particularly if system- or firm-level constraints impose substitution between internal and external activity. For instance, diseconomies of scale in production, such as increasing marginal costs, capacity constraints, or administrative limits to scale (Coase 1937), may promote plural forms (Puranam et al. 2013). In such cases, any increase in external sales may come at the expense of internally governed transfers. Such substitution in the firm boundary decision will generate a third testable pattern. External IT use will lead to increases in external transfers and decreases in internal transfers,significantly decreasing the ITR. Without additional information, the likelihoods of these competing propositions are ambiguous. Thus, the main net effect is ultimately an empirical question. 3.4. Heterogeneity and Mechanism Tests Countervailing mechanisms underlie these propositions and may be leveraged to deepen insight. Moreover, our interest in dynamics pushes us to investigate not justwhetherproduction chains tended to reorganize in response to the rise of the internet but alsowhy.As discussed, plural governance theory points to a few contingencies of interest. We recognize that in the absence of sharp main effect predictions, an “abductive” approach—one that characterizes potential explanations and then assesses their likely salience—is increasingly preferred, particularly in management research (King et al. 2021). Indeed, several of our tests, particularly with respect to different uses of IT, align with this style of investigation. Yet, given the theoretical importance of governance inseparability (Argyres and Liebeskind 2002), contractual complementarity (Novak and Stern 2009), cross-mode learning (Heide 2003,Anderson et al. 2019), production constraints (Puranam et al. 2013), and past value chain commitments (Jacobides and Hitt 2005) in the related literature, we draw out a few key contingences and their implications here before turning to the data. Our empirical approach takes the downstream vertical organization of U.S. manufacturing plants pre-internet as given. Thus, we do not seek to identify distinct drivers of plural governance in our data. This is primarily because our core research question centers on the dynamics arising from a rapid and far-reaching technological advance. In addition, core drivers of plural organization need not be mutually exclusive.9However, to the extent that differentorganizationaldrivers of plural governance play an important role in vertical production chain organization as described, we expect pre-internet plural selling, significant within-firm vertical linkages, and limited production capacity to each have the potential to amplify organizational responses toexternal ITthrough the channels of flexibility and cross-transaction complementarities and constraints. Further, to better understand the role of IT in shaping organizational design, we investigate interactions among different types ofinformation technologies, a few of which are easily motivated up front. For instance, given the high fixed costs of EDI adoption (Bakos and Nault 1997), we anticipate that it will act as a substitute forexternal IT, all else equal. With regard to internal uses of IT, cross-mode interactions in plural forms can give rise to outcomes that conflict with common TCE-based intuitions. A useful case in point is the aforementioned “Fashion Inc.” (Jacobides and Billinger 2006). If generalizable in a broader population, we should observe complementary interactions betweenexternal ITand ERP and for similar reasons, betweenexternal ITandproduction IT. We leave other IT interactions to post hoc exploration.
article.4. Data: Our analysis relies on a panel of detailed U.S. Census Bureau microdata collected at the plant level. The dependent variable and most controls come from the Annual Survey of Manufactures (ASM). Stratified by size and industry, with response required by law, the ASM captures ∼70% of sector-wide economic activity in the certainty subsample alone.10Establishment-level identifiers link to the 1999 CNUS describing plant use of networked computers and other IT (Atrostic and Nguyen 2005,Nguyen and Atrostic 2006) and to the 1992 and 2002 Census of Manufactures (CMF). Conducted every five years, the CMF covers the entire population of ∼300,000 manufacturing establishments in the country. Establishment and firm identifiers in the Longitudinal Business Database (LBD) link plants to their parent firms and grant visibility to firm-level changes in asset composition beyond what is visible in the ASM for which the sampling frame rotates every year ending in four or nine.11 Because our identification strategy requires plants be observable in both full census years, our results represent changes among plants that survived from 1992 to 2002. These tend to be larger and more vertically integrated than the overall population, on average; the mean ITR for plants belonging to multiunit firms (single units are always zero) is 12% in our analysis sample versus 5.4% in the 2002 CMF. Thus, our findings may be interpreted as most meaningful for the larger multiunit plants that also account for the lion’s share of activity in this sector. Given our interest in plural forms of organization, we take steps to identify plural-selling versus “pure”-type plants that could have systematically different responses to IT-driven changes in coordination. We thus flag vertically integrated “captive” plants that consistently transfer output exclusively within the firm in the preperiod as well as nonintegrated plants that sell exclusively outside the firm. We exclude from our initial sample the plants for which the ITR is either zero or above the 95th percentile for the three years available prior to the start of our estimation window. This leaves a core sample of approximately 5,600 establishments.Table 1reports descriptive statistics from 1992 and 2002 for key variables; Table A.1 in the Online Appendix presents their correlation matrix. Loggeneric IT capital expenditureand loginventory-to-sales ratioare winsorized at the 99th percentile. Census disclosure clearance date is May 21, 2018. N/A, not applicable. 4.1. Downstream Vertical Integration Our main dependent variable, theITR, is the value of output transferred from the plant to other units within the firm for “further assembly, fabrication, or manufacture” divided by total sales. The numerator directly captures output destined for further transformation in a vertically integrated production process. As such, it neatly distinguishes vertical integration from horizontal diversification, which is often difficult with other available measures (Hitt 1999,Villalonga 2004). Normalizing by total sales (the sum of internal and external transfers per the survey instructions) identifies the relative “internal intensity” of plural selling at the plant. It also reduces skewness and eases comparisons across plants of different sizes. This continuous ratio accounts for interdependencies between internal and external transfers that may affect ultimate organizational design. For example, if as inProposition 1, the value of internal transfers remains relatively stable while external transfers change, reduced vertical integration will be observable only by comparing such ratios before and afterexternal ITadoption. We explore changes in unscaled internal transfers and total sales separately to look for patterns consistent withProposition 2orProposition 3. The ITR measure varies more flexibly and frequently than the changes in facility ownership or industry participation used to study vertical reorganization in prior work. In the overall ASM, the percentage of value that stays within the firm is frequently zero and sometimes 100%, with every value in between. In our analysis sample, the mean ITR for 1992 is 14%, falling to 12% in 2002. There is also a great deal of year-over-year variance at the plant level, consistent with flexibility in plural governance. 4.2. IT Data To investigate the impact of an internet-driven change in coordination costs, we leverage the 1999 CNUS, which provides detailed data on the plant-level use of a variety of information technologies soon after the rise of the commercial internet in 1995. Specifically, it asks whether a particular type of information exchange is taking place with suppliers, customers, or other internal company units as well as what type of network the plant uses. The types of information covered are design specifications, product descriptions or catalog, demand projections, order status, production schedules, inventory data, and logistics or transportation. To facilitate interpretation and analysis, we construct indices ofexternal ITandinternal IT(see Online Appendix B for details). In our sample, 53% of establishments adopted some type of externally focused use of the internet by 1999.Table 1reports a mean value forexternal ITof 0.13, equivalent to nearly two “practices,” whereas the standard deviation of 0.19 is equivalent to nearly three practices. In contrast, the mean of theinternal ITindex is much higher at 0.36 or roughly five practices, with a likewise greater standard deviation of 0.38. This suggests greater penetration but also greater variation in cross-unit (internal) use of the internet by 1999. The CNUS also covers many uses of IT for production management, which we index to captureproduction IT; binary indicators for EDI and ERP are also available. Because these three measures do not rely on internet infrastructure, we can only rely on cross-sectional variation in these measures to identify their effects. Our only quantitative measure of IT investment comes from the ASM in the form of capital expenditures for computer and data-processing equipment, which the Census collects from 1992 onward (which we log in detail in Online Appendix B). A relevant consideration for plant-level analysis is the locus of decision-making authority for IT adoption. Although IT use is commonly measured at the firm level, prior work demonstrates that U.S. plants frequently have local discretion over IT purchases (McElheran 2014). Our data do not directly reveal where IT decisions are made. However, we can probe within-firm variation inexternal ITto gauge the extent of establishment-level discretion with respect to IT use. Specifically, for each plant in the CNUS, we identify the extensive margin of any type ofexternal ITbeing used and then compute the fraction of CNUS establishments12within each firm with at least some use.Figure 1shows the distribution of firms in our sample arranged by quartile according to the percentage of a firm’s facilities withexternal IT. Intermediate levels of usage dominate: 66% have within-firm usage rates between 20% and 60%. In contrast, only 17% have rates between 0% and 20%, and only 4% exceed 80%. From this, we infer that modeling IT use at the establishment level is both conceptually and empirically sound. 4.3. Controls A particular concern for identifying the effect of shifts in coordination costs is whether the ITR might shift instead because of internal productivity improvements (Hitt 1999,Jacobides and Hitt 2005). Although we view this as an important mechanism to include in our assessment of how IT ultimately affects vertical design, we also explore the implications of partialing out improvements in a plant’s productivity. We leverage an estimate of plant total factor productivity (TFP) provided in the ASM (Cooper et al. 1999). We also control for overall firm composition (including nonmanufacturing establishments) with the logged count of all sites belonging to the firm (in the LBD).13Because both TFP and firm composition may be endogenously related to IT use, we verify that all reported results are robust to their exclusion. Each plural-selling plant is embedded within a larger set of transactions within and between firms. Although precisely mapping a given plant’s value chain network is beyond the reach of even Census data, we are nevertheless able to control for changes in a plant’s downstream demand both within the firm and in the external market (see Online Appendix B). We also include two controls for the presence of competition in the plant’s primary industry (seeRay et al. 2009). Other plant-level and time-varying controls include on-hand inventory (Milgrom and Roberts 1988), employee skill mix (Fichman and Melville 2014), and complexity of the production process proxied by the number of products (Forbes and Lederman 2009).
article.5. Empirical Approach: FollowingSection 3, we wish to understand the impact of IT investments arising from the introduction of the commercial internet on the relative intensity of ownership-based control over vertical transfers while allowing for cross-transaction complements and constraints. Given that we observe annual data, we aggregate shipments and internal transfers by plant-year, focusing on the share of the value destined for within the firm. Using thisITR, we estimate the following linear model 5.1. Timing Our start and end dates represent the complete census years bracketing the introduction and diffusion of the commercial internet. Critically, our panel starts before the internet is available, soEXTERNAL_ITin (1) is zero for all plants in 1992. This provides a clean “pretreatment” period before various applications appear to exploit the internet’s new open platform. The CNUS survey is for 1999; by then, the diffusion of internet use was well under way, particularly across the larger plants prominent in our sample. We thus observe differences in IT adoption that took place between the introduction of the internet in 1995 and 1999 across the manufacturing sector. Significant IT-related changes are widely understood to appear with a lag (Brynjolfsson and Hitt 2003,Tambe and Hitt 2012). Thus, we take the next full economic census in 2002 as a useful end date for our analysis sample. Not only does this allow for potentially critical organizational adjustment to take place, it also grants visibility to the universe of U.S. manufacturing plants in both years, yielding the largest and most representative panel possible, including all key controls (seeSection 4and Online Appendix B). 5.2. Functional Form Note that the dependent variable is bounded by zero and one; thus, we view our core estimation results as a linear approximation to an underlying nonlinear model. We also further examine the robustness of our results to alternative nonlinear approaches that make a different set of parametric assumptions, in particular panel fractional probit and Tobit models. SeeSection 6. 5.3. Endogeneity and Causal Interpretation Our approach shares similarities with prior work that estimates the relationship between IT and firm boundaries by regressing IT investments on firm boundary decisions (e.g.,Hitt 1999,Ray et al. 2009). Strong assumptions are typically needed to interpret this relationship as causal, and we address the standard concerns with instrumental variables (IV) estimation, timing falsification tests, and other tests of the robustness of a causal interpretation.

Although the direction of the estimated effect is stable across specifications, magnitudes are generally larger than the comparable ordinary least squares estimates, and significances vary. We speculate that the difference in estimates across the two models may be, in part, because of heterogeneous effects of external IT on internal transfers; that is, overall, the local average treatment effect forexternal ITuse may be largest for plants that are most influenced by competitor adoption of IT, IT-related capabilities within the firm, and the local costs of delivering telecommunications services. Overall, this supports a causal interpretation thatexternal ITuse led to a decline in the percentage of internal transfers; however, the average treatment effect is difficult to pin down because of heterogeneity in compliance across establishments. 6.3. Robustness: Timing Falsification, Nonlinear Models, and Additional Controls A standard identification concern in studies involving IT adoption is reverse causality (Tambe and Hitt 2012), which is addressed by the IV estimation. However, we are able to further probe the timing of organizational and technological changes in our panel setting. A timing falsification exercise, where we examine whether observedexternal ITadoption in 1999 was associated with changes in ITR thatprecededthe introduction of the commercial internet, further supports a causal interpretation of the patterns inTable 2(see the discussion in Online Appendix B and columns (1)–(4) in Table A.4 in the Online Appendix). Moreover, we find our results to be robust to other functional forms, such as nonlinear fractional response models and one-sided and two-sided Tobit models. Both approaches yield consistent patterns (discussed in Online Appendix B and reported in columns (5)–(8) in Table A.4 in the Online Appendix). Another alternative explanation is the potential for unobserved changes in the firm composition (i.e., firm composition changes not addressed by our controls inTable 2) to affect both IT use and local plant-level governance decisions. Adoption and use of IT systems have been shown to influence the likelihood of mergers and acquisitions (M&A) (Benitez et al. 2018,Cao et al. 2022).External ITand other margins of IT could, therefore, be correlated with M&A, and by changing firm structure, M&A transactions could influence plant-level ITR, creating an omitted variable bias. We, therefore, explore the robustness of our results to additional controls for changes in parent firm structure. The ASM contains detailed information linking establishments to their firms, and through linkages to the LBD, we are able to observe how these change over time. Using these data, we construct three controls: (1) whether the plant has changed firms between 1992 and 2002, (2) the percentage of other establishments in the focal plant’s firm in 1992 that have changed firms by 2002 (only if observed in 2002), and (3) the growth in the size (measured by number of establishments) of the focal plant’s 1992 firm between 1992 and 2002 (only if nonzero in 2002). The results, included in Tables A.5 and A.6 in the Online Appendix, show that our main findings are extremely robust to the inclusion of these variables. 6.4.
