Plural vs. “Pure” Types. Column (1) inTable 4mimics column (3) inTable 2, but it includes in the sample plants that consistently either never or almost always transferred output outside the firm in the presample period. These are “pure”-type plants that can be characterized discretely, either “integrated” in the sense of transferring output only to other units in the firm or “not-integrated” (i.e., using the market for all vertical transactions). The magnitude of the point estimate forexternal ITgoes down—by roughly one half—once these nonplural plants are included in the sample. In column (2) inTable 4, in the same less restricted sample, we interact the index of external internet uses with an indicator for plants that consistently pursued plural selling in the presample period. Here, we observe that all of the reduction in the ITR associated withexternal ITarose among the plurally governed plants. This is consistent with greater flexibility to adapt to external shocks in plural organizational forms (e.g.,Chondrakis et al. 2022), as discussed inSection 3. 6.4.2. Internal Vertical Linkages. Column (3) inTable 4explores the importance of considering another dimension of the ex ante vertical organization of production within the firm: significant internal vertical linkages across production units within the firm. Returning to our main sample, we construct an indicator for plants that have a potentially important “internal customer” somewhere within the same firm prior to the internet. To calculate this vertical interdependence, we followAtalay et al. (2014)to identify plants whose output is used by other plants within the firm as part of a nontrivial link in the vertical production chain. Such a link is presumed to exist between a plant in industry A and a plant in industry B when industry A produces a commodity constituting at least 5%19of the intermediate materials purchased by firms in industry B according to the 2002 Benchmark Input-Output tables (https://www.bea.gov/). We observe in column (3) inTable 4that declines in the ITR associated with external internet use are concentrated among plants that had a nontrivial pre-existing vertical linkage to another unit within the parent firm. This pattern could arguably be counterintuitive without the plural governance perspective. Consider that these plants have a greater demand for internal coordination, pre-internet (e.g.,McElheran 2014). Further, vertical interdependence in production is considered to be among the most costly organizational designs to coordinate (Thompson 1967,Dewan and Ren 2011). Thus, this approach identifies a subset of plants whose organization of transactions pre-internet entailed significant internal coordination costs. If this were optimal, for instance because of strategic needs to integrate or inability to coordinate vertically without co-ownership of facilities (e.g.,Novak and Stern 2009), one would expect anincreasein internal activity once internet-based coordination relaxed the coordination constraint. Instead, easing coordination costs led to significant reductions in downstream vertical integration. This is consistent with strong cross-transaction interdependencies in plurally governed organizations (Puranam et al. 2013), as discussed inSection 3. Other constraints could also be at work, including past commitments and organizational inertia (Jacobides and Hitt 2005). These potential drivers of plural forms need not be mutually exclusive, and disentangling them is beyond the scope of our study. This heterogeneity test, however, reinforces our interpretation ofTable 2; if adoption ofexternal ITprimarily only increased sales (Proposition 1, the “denominator-only” effect), the impact should be observable outside this subpopulation; however, the uninteracted coefficient is small and imprecise. 6.4.3. Constraints on Scaling. Column (4) inTable 4explores the effect of physical constraints on scaling production, another hypothesized driver of plural governance. Here, we take advantage of the U.S. Census Bureau’s Plant Capacity Utilization (PCU) Survey to observe production constraints in the presample period.20An indicator of high capacity utilization—greater than 75% in this specification—amplifies the effect of external internet use. Although the interaction term is only significant at the 10% level, the linear combination for more-constrained plants yields a coefficient of −0.117 and is significant at the 1% level. This coefficient is −0.140 and significant at the 1% level for plants that were using 80% or more of their preperiod available capacity (not reported for brevity). Two observations are in order. First, the direct effect ofexternal ITin this specification is significant. This suggests that less-constrained plants (i.e., those with greater excess capacity) leveraged the internet to become less vertically integrated. This is consistent with facilities having larger minimum efficient scale using enhanced external coordination to find supply-side economies of scale outside the firm (Harrigan 1985,Kapoor 2013). A “denominator-only” effect (Proposition 1) may be the most appropriate explanation for how vertical integration declines in this subgroup. Second, however, the large negative interaction terms suggests that among plants where production scaling was most constrained, external transfers may have come at the expense of internal transfers, more consistent withProposition 3. 6.4.4. Robustness and Heterogeneity in IT Usage. We cannot directly observe coordination costs—nor how they shift with the use of internet-based IT. However, we gain insight by examining different approaches to between-firm coordination. Column (5) inTable 4splitsexternal ITby whether specific uses represent the sharing of static versus dynamic information. Specifically, we designate the sharing of design and product specifications or catalog information as “static,” whereas we designate logistics or transportation, on-hand inventory, production schedules, order status, or demand projections as “dynamic.” Although the former can still have coordination benefits, we conjecture that sharing real-time information about supply and demand will more directly mitigate costly coordination of vertically linked activities having to operate without the control benefits of co-ownership. Indeed, we observe thatdynamiccoordination over the internet has a stronger association with declines in the ITR, consistent with this mechanism. Column (6) inTable 4explores robustness to another dimension of how we defineexternal IT. Although prior work emphasizes the benefits of coordinating both upstream and downstream in production chains (e.g.,Simchi-Levi et al. 1999,Lee and Whang 2000), column (6) inTable 4shows that our core results are robust to excluding upstream coordination from theexternal ITindex. The coefficient onexternal ITwhen it includes only measures of downstream-focused (customer-only) coordination is −0.046, a slightly lower point estimate but within the 95% confidence interval for the coefficient in column (3) inTable 2. The next three columns test the intuitions developed inSection 3.4regarding heterogeneity based on other uses of IT. Recall that for all three of these IT measures, we cannot exploit a clean pre- and post-internet shift in this measure as it is not dependent on the new internet infrastructure; thus, these effects are identified off of cross-sectional variation. Column (7) inTable 4explores the extent to which internet-based coordination substitutes for earlier technologies—in particular, EDI. The interaction effect between EDI and internet-basedexternal ITis positive, indicating that plants already on an EDI network demonstrated less of a response to new internet-based IT, as anticipated: the linear combination of the two is smaller than the other main effects (i.e., column (3) inTable 2) and only significant at the 10% level. Column (8) inTable 4reports on interactions with ERP. Although we had anticipated a positive interaction, in line with the “Fashion Inc.” case study (Jacobides and Billinger 2006), the interaction effect is not statistically significant in our setting. That said, we had similarly anticipated a positive interaction betweenexternal ITand production technology aimed at making shop-floor production more productive—and possibly more responsive to external demand. Column (9) inTable 4shows that almost all of the effect ofexternal ITcan be found among plants that were also using IT for internal production management. The linear combination is large at −0.094 and significant at the 1% level. This is consistent with the importance of productive capabilities influencing vertical scope (Hitt 1999,Jacobides and Hitt 2005) and complementarities between certain types of internal and external IT in the firm boundary decision. 6.4.5. Robustness in Firm-Level Analysis. Although our focal analyses do not seek to estimate the aggregate impact ofexternal ITacross the entire firm, the plants we track across this pivotal decade are embedded within broader firm contexts. However, data limitations constrain our ability to incorporate this broader context by conducting a firm-level analysis. Recall that our sample consists of plants that exist across both years of our data, have received the long survey of the ASM in 1992 and 2002 (which allows us to observe internal plant transfers), and have received the CNUS survey in 1999. As a result of this approach, we do not observe activity from all establishments within a firm—nor even all of its manufacturing establishments. Moreover, for those units we do observe in at least one year, entry and exit make it difficult to identify the effects ofexternal ITon transaction flows at the firm level using our baseline two-period model. Given these constraints, we can only explore the robustness of our findings to a firm-level analysis by exploiting cross-sectional variance in our 2002 data to assess the effects ofexternal ITacross a broader set of establishments. Results are presented in Table A.7 in the Online Appendix, and sample construction details and discussion may be found in Online Appendix B. On average, we find a much noisier relationship betweenexternal ITand downstream vertical integration when aggregated to the firm level. Such an approach obscures the margins of variation underlying the establishment-level findings and points to the importance of the unit-level approach in plural governance research (seeSection 2.4). Heterogeneity among firms can be observed, however, in line with our original predictions; firms composed largely of plural-selling establishments pre-internet show the same digitally enabled reorganization away from vertical integration. This again points to greater flexibility among plurally governed organizations and the importance of past organizational design choices.
article.7. Discussion and Conclusion: This study explores IT-related changes in the vertical organization of production chains from a new perspective that complements prior work by combining plural governance theories of the firm with unusually detailed administrative data at the establishment level, both of which are novel to this literature. This approach yields new insights into how digital coordination may substitute for ownership in the vertical organization of production in modern firms. In a large sample of plants representing the majority of economic activity across the sector, we find a robust causal relationship between internet-enhanced external coordination and a decline in downstream vertical integration in U.S. manufacturing in the early 2000s. Levels of market-based activity not only increased, but also, external sales rose at the expense of within-firm transfers, consistent with our predictions. A number of pre-internet organizational design features further augmented the observed vertical integration declines on net as did certain uses of internal IT. Our exploration of heterogeneity arising from ex ante organizational design required the development of new intuitions regarding IT and vertical integration, extending beyond classic TCE-based predictions. Recognition has grown in the past 20 years that vertical integration decisions will be shaped not only by transaction-level features but also, by cross-transaction interdependencies and past design choices. Although this may incorporate certain TCE-based mechanisms (e.g.,Argyres and Zenger 2012), a growing body of work calls for a richer view of organizational design and dynamics—a call we answer in this study. Plural governance both fits our empirical context and leverages these advances in the theory of the firm to shed light on how interdependencies across governance modes and time periods may amplify organizational adjustments to shocks, such as the internet. Testing these more nuanced predictions, we found that plants that were plurally governed before the rise of the internet demonstrated greater responsiveness toexternal IT, consistent with greater flexibility in plural forms despite the higher-cost organizational design. This is useful not only for understanding dynamics in organizational design (Nickerson and Silverman 2003,Argyres and Bigelow 2010,Anderson et al. 2019) but also, for learning which organizational features may help or hinder adjustment in the face of novel IT. This is of rising interest as digital transformation becomes a topic of urgent managerial attention (Bharadwaj et al. 2013,Westerman et al. 2014,Vial 2019). We also found—perhaps surprisingly—that some of the most-constrained plants demonstrated the greatest net adjustments. Plants with significant ex ante vertical interdependencies within the firm or with limited available production capacity more aggressively shifted away from ownership-based governance of their downstream transactions. This is consistent with cross-mode interdependencies arising from organizational constraints and/or “dual-mode” capabilities that shaped the overall system of transactions once external coordination costs were reduced. The plural governance frame is essential to explaining these patterns in the results. Finally, extending plural governance intuitions to examine interdependencies among different uses of IT yielded new insights as well. In contrast to prior findings of substitution (Dewan and Ren 2011,Rai et al. 2015), we found that internally focused production IT reinforced the effect of internet-based external coordination, consistent with complementarity in the firm boundary decision. Cross-unit coordination via the internet within the firm had relatively no effect, likely because of asymmetries in how the widespread and open internet platform shifted internal versus external coordination compared with pre-internet IT. The potential for IT to alter the vertical organizational design of firms by lowering information and coordination costs has been a topic of vital interest across management and economics research (Malone et al. 1987;Gurbaxani and Whang 1991;Hitt 1999;Afuah 2003;Baker and Hubbard 2003,2004;Ray et al. 2009;Dewan and Ren 2011, inter alia). Yet, although other aspects of IT and firm structure have been explored, such as M&A (Benitez et al. 2018,Cao et al. 2022) and alliances (Tafti et al. 2013,Liu and Ravichandran 2015,Ravichandran and Giura 2019), the relationship between IT and ownership-based control of vertical production has received comparatively little attention of late—despite dramatic changes in both IT and production chain (including supply chain) organization in recent decades. Consider, first, the nature and rate of innovation in IT. The rise of the commercial internet rapidly ushered in a widespread availability of open, low-cost communication standards and greater ease of use across a broad range of IT applications. Its implications extend far beyond our study, which focuses on the internet’s initial diffusion in the mid-1990s and early 2000s. Yet, by examining the introduction of this paradigm shift in IT, we can more cleanly identify its effects, including disentangling distinct margins of use and ruling out a large number of alternative explanations. The common absence of a distinct “pretreatment” period, not to mention systematic measures, for represent ongoing challenges for empirical studies of the economic effects of innovative IT (e.g.,McElheran 2018). Further, by directly examining vertical transfers in interdependent production processes, we shed light on a margin of structural reorganization that has been inaccessible in standard data sets. In particular, the production chain reorganization we observe took place without costly changes in facility ownership. This flexible and more continuous verticaldis-integrationand the extent to which it may have grown with accelerating digitization subsequent to our data are important questions in need of further investigation. Our study also sheds light on mechanisms with important managerial implications. Notably, we find that plurally governed plants were disproportionately responsive to changes in IT use, suggesting that this organizational design may ease the response to IT-related shocks. Although “pure” organizational types may have been less responsive for strategic reasons beyond the reach of our data, our study suggests that the dynamic response to novel IT use has deep organizational roots. Practically speaking, therefore, managers seeking to exploit innovations in IT should consider whether the design of control mechanisms within the organization (e.g., the extent of vertical integration) supports IT-related objectives. This a specific example of more general arguments in favor of strategic alignment between features of the organizational context and desired uses of IT (e.g.,Bharadwaj et al. 2013,Mithas et al. 2013). Even more concretely, we find that dynamic coordination (related to logistics, order status, or on-hand inventory) and specific combinations of external IT and internal production technology disproportionately eased external coordination challenges. This aligns with a growing recognition that particular applications of IT may be more important than sheer levels of investment (e.g.,Dewan and Ren 2011). Evolution in production chain organization and management has proceeded alongside advances in IT. Recognition is now quite widespread that value chains have become more dispersed, lean, and dependent on nonownership control mechanisms to align and coordinate supply and demand across time and space. Our study points to a causal relationship between this trend and the use of internet-based IT. That said, strict causation (running from IT use to changes in production chain organization) may not be the only relationship of interest—particularly as cycles of digitization accelerate. Understanding complementary and reinforcing cycles of digital and organizational innovation is becoming increasingly important as rates of change accelerate (e.g.,Brynjolfsson et al. 2021). In response, a burgeoning research area seeks to understand the relationship between IT and organizations through the lens of complementarities (e.g.,Bresnahan et al. 2002,Tambe et al. 2012,Puranam et al. 2013,Brynjolfsson and McElheran 2016,Wu et al. 2019,Brynjolfsson et al. 2021). If payoffs from one organizational decision are increasing in the presence of another, their co-movement is inherently informative about how shifts in the cost of one complement (e.g., information exchange) should impact another (e.g., organizational design). From this perspective, our results can be viewed as an example of the “correlation” test for complementarities (Brynjolfsson and Milgrom 2013) between IT and organizational design. How this may affect firm performance—and variation in returns to IT use—is another vital question for future investigation. Finally, our study brings downstream vertical organization into focus, despite an overwhelming focus on upstream “make versus buy” decisions in prior work. It also highlights the importance of heterogeneity across plants as well as across distinct uses of IT. These distinctive features of our approach shed new light on how firms adopt, combine, and adapt to novel technologies. Certain limitations of our study are worth bearing in mind. First, we acknowledge that any study that relies on observational data must be cautious with causal interpretations, and although we address a large number of standard identification concerns, there are limits to what is possible, even in the detailed U.S. Census Bureau data. In addition, we cannot rule out unobserved heterogeneity at an even lower level of aggregation (e.g., at the transaction level) that may be worth exploring and may yield distinct empirical and theoretical implications. Further, our econometric approach and limitations of the data (e.g., the timing of complete census years) require that plants be observed over a 10-year difference. This may impose a survivor bias on our findings, suggesting care in assuming that these effects hold equally for new or at-risk firms that do not persist across our sample. Finally, our empirical setting is confined to the manufacturing sector and to a particular point in time when the diffusion of internet-based IT was quite rapid. Thus, the extent to which these findings generalize to other sectors and other generations of technology over time warrants additional study. Despite these limitations, our study forges new connections between underutilized theories of the firm and novel data to understand the relationship between IT use and organizational design. Ultimately, our approach opens up more new avenues of inquiry than it resolves, and much work remains to be done. We hope our findings inspire further exploration of these far-ranging and increasingly important phenomena.
